speaker: interviewer
text: --NY. This is Elsbeth Lewin, January 6-- January 4, 1996, tape two. I'd like to ask you about your home, as far as Judaism is concerned. Would you consider yourself a religious Jew, and did you come from an orthodox home?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't come from an orthodox home. I came what I would consider a conservative home. In other words, we did not keep kosher, but we did keep the Shabbat. Especially after I started going to the Jewish school and had a lot more Jewish education than I would have had than if I'd gone to another school. And we kept the holidays. We went to services on, well, some Shabbat, but mostly on the holidays. And in those days on the holidays, the men wore stovepipe hats in Germany. Yes, they wore this only to two kinds of occasions, to the holidays and-- you know, I can't imagine going to a Yom Kippur service and have this stiff hat on all the time. And they also wore them to funerals. And I can remember that around the eighth of November, 1938, a good friend of my grandmother's died. And my father said, tomorrow on the ninth of November, bring-- these hats were kept in the synagogue, in the cloakroom of the synagogue. And he said, tomorrow when you go to school, bring home our cylinder, is what it was called. They were cylinders. I mean, these stovepipe hats, silk hats, opera hats. Whatever you want to call them. I forgot. And I said, well, the funeral isn't until the next day. I'll bring them home the next day. Well, the next day there was no more synagogue. There was no more school. There was no more stovepipe hats. That was day of Kristallnacht.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the hats perished with the synagogue. They were the least valuable part of it. But all I'm saying-- you know, reconnecting the high holidays. I mean, the men were downstairs. It was a magnificent synagogue. What was the name of the synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was called Hauptsynagoge, which means the chief synagogue. And it had been built in-- it had been dedicated in 1912. For the ages, they thought. It was built of marble and gold, and it had an enormous chandelier. And it had a beautiful round kodesh, and bema, and maybe 12 or more Sefer Torah, each of which had elaborate golden breast plates, and rimonim, and yad for reading. And it was-- it just-- it was like an opera house. In fact, some people thought it was a bit too much. And you know, if you went up the stairs, it was like going up to the opera house. And up the stairs, were in the tier of what would be an opera house, a horseshoe where the women sat. And I used to sit with my mother and grandmother. But when we went to school later on, to the Jewish school, they made space for us down for all the children, downstairs with the men. And so we sat-- the children sat downstairs together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you happen to remember the name of your rabbi in the Hauptsynagoge?

speaker: interviewee
text: Our rabbi was Sali Levi-- S-A-L-I L-E-V-I. He was a magnificent man. He was handsome with a gray beard. He was an artist as well as a rabbi. He came originally from a very small town in southern Germany, but going to a rabbinical school in Breslow, where my husband is from. And where there was a very important rabbinical school. He served as a chaplain during the First World War, and was just a wonderful, dedicated man. He was the co-director of our school and stayed on even though he had-- had several chances to come to this country. He stayed with his congregation until almost the end, when he went to Berlin to immigrate and had a heart attack there. Which was a great loss. He was not an old man. I thought he was old, but I think he was in his 40s or 50s at the time. I'm still in touch with his daughter, who is a few years older than I. And I remember his son, too. And his daughter is a very gifted artist and poet, and lives in California.

speaker: interviewer
text: So would you say, then, that the Hauptsynagoge was conservative?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was conservative. We did-- I mean, the men wore hats, if that's what you want to-- you know, one way of judging it. But it was not-- it was not an orthodox. And it was close to reform, but I don't think it was that kind of reform in Germany at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you celebrate the Jewish-- all the Jewish holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Well we celebrated, we certainly had a wonderful Seder at my grandparents' house. And we had Hanukkah, and we had-- well, in school we had Purim. And of course, the high holidays. We observed very, very assiduously with fasting and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your favorite holiday?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think Seder. I think of Pesach. It's-- I think it's everybody's favorite holiday. It's so special. And I mean, as a child, the high holidays can get kind of boring. And so you-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But you know, Purim and Hanukkah, and we observed Shavuot. And there was-- we had the confirmation on Shavuot. I remember, I must've been about 14, and we were-- when the class was confirmed. I did not have a bat mitzvah. Those-- we didn't have bat mitzvahs in those days. But the boys had bar mitzvahs, of course. But I didn't hae-- I'm an only child. But I remember my cousins' bar mitzvahs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your parents ever talk about how they met? Did they both meet in Mainz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they met at a B'nai B'rith function. My mother had-- my mother's brother and his wife lived in Mainz. So that was the next largest town to which my mother would gravitate from her little town. She couldn't wait to get-- get away from that little town. And so she stayed for weeks on end. So they went to this big party given in a B'nai B'rith function. And they met there. And they had a rather interesting courtship, because there was the French occupation in the early '20s. And he had to get a-- a permit every time he wanted to come to see her. And the same thing, even if she wanted to go away from her little town, she had to get some sort of permit. In French, yet, which I don't think either of them spoke that well. And so they had a meeting place which they talk about, to which, however, they had to have a chaperone to come with them. I think sometimes they just took a dog. [LAUGHS] But it was difficult times, because also the inflation was rampant at the time. And when they had finally set their wedding date, their-- apparently, their correspondence consisted, you know, buy this kind of soap, because you can now get it, and later on you can't. And now it's cheaper here, or there. And the other thing is in those days, women still had a dowry. And my mother's dowry vanished in the inflation. I mean, it just boom, it just went. And as a matter of fact, when my grandmother made an informal will, she stipulated that my mother get an extra portion because she didn't get enough dowry at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were married in what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were married in 1923. And actually, my father's stomach ulcer acted up. He contracted it during the war when his two brothers were killed, lots of stress. And so he had to have an operation on the day before the wedding was scheduled. And my mother's mother arrived, and there was no wedding. And so when they rescheduled it, she had scarlet fever. My mother's mother had scarlet fever, my grandmother. So she never got to that wedding.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they had a-- I gather what was rather small wedding, I think in my father's parents' home. But well, what can I tell you? I was born nine months later. [LAUGHS] And then you were an only-- you were an only child?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was an only-- yeah, that scared them off. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So you lived-- you lived in what you considered a conservative home. You did celebrate the holidays.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you did go to a Jewish school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to talk a little bit about the political climate in Germany between the years of 1933 and 1939. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Was politics talked about in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, if it was, it was not-- it was talked about very carefully, because it was felt that the walls had ears. As long as there was a maid in the house, it was-- conversation stopped with the minute the maid came in. Even children, it was felt that the children might overhear something that they would repeat outside of the house. And so I think, while my parents weren't very political-- which is unfortunately the way a lot of middle class people were in Weimar Germany. They felt politics was a dirty business, and they'd rather not be bothered. They voted, but they would rather not be bothered. And maybe if more people had cared, we might not have come to the past where Hitler came to power.

speaker: interviewer
text: But-- and in fact, when I was in Germany some couple of years ago, and there was an official discussion on this. And I said, be sure you get involved in politics and vote. And don't let this kind of thing happen again. They were concerned at the time in Germany about how to avoid all this nastiness with foreigners that was going on at the time-- a couple of years in Germany, a couple of years ago. And so they said, well, what can you recommend? So that's what I recommended, for whatever good it may do. So in a sense, you're saying that the apathy of-- of the people in general, or Jews in particular Germany led to--

speaker: interviewee
text: I think the middle class in particular. The people who were thrown out of work, they were political, obviously, for obvious reasons. They were either Nazis or communists. But a lot of the middle class was just apathetic. And I can't say whether it wouldn't have happened. I mean, that's not for me to say. I was a child, and who knows what I would have done? But I just felt that it was-- well, let me go back and say this. It was discussed at home. And my parents felt things were going to get bad. They never knew how bad they were going to get. But as a child, I was rather protected from this. In the same sense that I was protected in school, that I had friends. And once I was in the Jewish school, we had our little community. And there was no-- not this present sense of danger that I now, as an adult, feel when I read about it. When I read how things got worse and worse progressively. And people were apprehended on the street, and were mistreated, and for no reason at all except that they were Jewish. And businesses were closed, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you witness people being pulled off the streets and sent away?

speaker: interviewee
text: I personally didn't, no. But my parents did. And they knew what happened. And again, this was not discussed in front of the-- of the children. [HEBREW] was the password.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you translate?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's Jewish for be quiet, the children are here, are listening.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- did you belong to any organizations-- Zionist organizations, or political organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: I belonged to an organization called Bund Deutsch-judischer Jugend. You don't have to spell it. It means [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Organization of German Jewish Youth. And we did what German young people did, which was sing, and hike, and you know, there was probably a little Jewish indoctrination in it. It was definitely not Zionist. My parents did not subscribe to the Zionist point of view. It was very new then, you have to remember. There was very little chance that there ever would be an independent Eretz Yisrael. And so in fact, none of my close family emigrated to Israel. Frank has a cousin there. I have nobody. Of course, I didn't have many cousins. That's-- that was one thing. But there was never any thought of-- and you know, they weren't opposed to it. It's just, it was not part of our interest, let's put it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as things began to get-- to go from bad to worse, there was never any discussion in your home about going to Palestine, or leaving?

speaker: interviewee
text: Going to America. Going to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: Only to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only to America. I mean, eventually-- in 1934, when I started in this new school, and we had an excellent English teacher. And all the emphasis was on English. This teacher had taught in the high school, the one that I took the exam to go to. She had taught there for years, Fraulein Kahn, Sophie Kahn. And had an excellent way of teaching. And this was-- the whole emphasis was on learn English, because you'll probably wind up in America. And my father did have a number of cousins in this country. So we had connections. As it turned out, it was difficult still to get an affidavit of support, because of the depression here in the '30s. But the whole idea was if we have to go someplace, we'll go to America. And then the question, the well-known question of getting a visa number came up. And my father, I guess-- oh, you know, one of the times he was in Frankfurt, l he got a visa number. And it was for 1940. On the Kristallnacht, which I guess you're going to come to, he went to Frankfurt again and got one for his parents. And it was for a year later. And of course, that was already too late. And the reason-- one reason that we didn't immigrate earlier is A, the business has been quite good up to that point. B, the parents were-- my mother's parents had died by 1933. My father's parents were elderly. We would have-- they would have had to be supported. They would've-- it would've been very difficult for them to move. Then the other thing is-- well, for one thing, we felt it would blow over. And you don't leave the country of your birth lightly, even if it had been easy to make another career. You live where you live. You have-- you have your roots there. Literally roots. I mean, some of the books that I have just been going through express it extremely well. I mean, this-- you've lived there-- your family's lived there for hundreds and hundreds of years. You have no reason to think that because some house painter from Austria is coming along that you have to leave. And so [LAUGHS] you wait until it's almost too late. And a lot of people waited until it was too late.

speaker: interviewer
text: I have to ask you, how was-- how was Nazism perceived by both the non-Jewish community and the Jewish community within Mainz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there are-- there are parts of Nazism which appeal to the people who had been through a depression, through a lost war. He promised revenge for the lost war, and he promised jobs for everyone, which he managed to do by building armaments and so on. So all of a sudden, there weren't any unemployed anymore. So it was-- there was a lot of positive perception on the part of the general populace.

speaker: interviewer
text: As far as the Jewish community was concerned, they were immediately aware that this was a dangerous thing. But as I said, they thought it would blow over. They never in their wildest dream-- I mean, who would've thought it could have come to extermination, and to all the things that it came to? I mean, you just-- in-- in the 19th-- in the 20th century, these things didn't happen anymore, and certainly not in a civilized country like Germany. I mean, this was the country of Goethe and of Beethoven. You know, you can't believe that this is going to happen. So in a sense, what you're saying is that they functioned in denial?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it wasn't-- it was denial in the sense, if they could have foreseen the future, it would have been denial. But they didn't foresee just how bad it was going to get. I mean, yes, they said, this can't last forever. Something's going to happen that maybe the outside nations-- England, France, will interfere. And the night of Kristallnacht, I remember we listened to the radio. We got shortwave radio. After all, that's not-- France was not very far away. Luxembourg broadcast in German. And they mentioned that this had happened, and it had happened on the anniversary of the Peace of 1918. And instead of celebrating the peace, this is what the-- what the Nazis did. But nobody interfered. And you know, the European nations didn't. Obviously, what's happening in Bosnia now. I mean, it-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was internal business. You couldn't-- they couldn't interfere. And certainly, nobody was much interested in interfering for the-- for the good of the Jews. I mean, that's-- certainly America wasn't, United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was nobody really interested in interfering. And so I don't know what they hoped for, what miracle they hoped for would make it go away. Prior to Kristallnacht, did you ever feel as a child threatened by the changes that were occurring within your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as a child, I wouldn't say threatened. I was-- as I said, I was very protected. What we-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know how to put it. We couldn't go to the theater. We couldn't go to the movies. We couldn't go to the public-- it's called [GERMAN]-- the public baths with swimming pools, which were really floats on the Rhine. I mean the Rhine was a clean river in those days. And-- and they were just big because floating things. Organized, you know, with little dressing rooms and stuff like that. But we were no-- everywhere there was a sign saying, [GERMAN], which means, Jews are not wanted here. And the merchants had to put it up, whether they wanted or not. For their own sake, they have to put up these signs if they wanted to stay on the good side of the authorities.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was really-- again, we had to make our own entertainment. We swam. We took our bicycles, And we went out to a peninsula on the Rhine. And we swam, you know, without the-- the paraphernalia. You just-- we just jumped in the Rhine and swam, which is not un-dangerous. I would hate to have my children or grandchildren do it. And I know my parents were very ambivalent about it. But they didn't want to keep me from being with my friends, and we were all together and looking out for each other.

speaker: interviewee
text: But these were the little things that you expect children, you know, you expect to be able to do, to go swimming, and to go to the theater, and to the opera, and so on and so forth. Sneaked into the theater and the opera a couple of times. Because well, as a child, people didn't know me, you know. So I would go with a cousin or something. And I remember seeing, I think, and and so on. But that was very unofficial. My parents couldn't go anymore. They had their own, organized their own, what was called a kulturbund, which was a group of people. They came-- came lectures, and singers, and concerts came in. And so they had that kind of entertainment, which was still first class in those days.

speaker: interviewee
text: But so did I feel threatened? Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] yes. There was some unease. There was unease of, you know, if somebody wanted to haunt me, taunt me, they had the freedom to do it. And maybe once or twice, yes, there was. Never anybody I knew, but if they thought I was Jewish. And on Kristallnacht-- well, I don't know if you want me to talk about Kristallnacht. Is this the appropriate time? I'd like to just ask you before we get into Kristallnacht, if you look back and you think about that time, do you recall any opposition to the rise of Nazism within Mainz?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't political. I mean, as Jews, we were opposed. But we couldn't-- you know, I mean, there was nothing we could do. And occasionally somebody would come by and say, you know, what can we do about this? Or you know, which they-- you know. There was-- well, there was talk. But I don't know whether there was any opposition, because this was such an overpowering phenomenon all of a sudden. That the minute you were in the opposition, you were suspect. You could get arrested. You could get thrown in concentration camp. And you'd-- and certainly, people weren't willing to go out on a limb for that sort of thing. So there may be people who-- maybe people who are older than I am might recall that. I do not, as an organized way.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll continue on the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I was wondering how we--
